name: how to differentiate in mixed ability classrooms
top_issues: C10 (Wayground Value Prop) scores a 2: The CTA section is almost entirely generic — 'Import your next unit, run a quick pre-check, and assign just-right tasks with built-in accommodations' names zero specific Wayground features by their actual product name. Compare to the C10 rubric standard: 'Wayground's Create with AI lets you generate tiered activities in 3 minutes.' This article never gets close to that. The phrase 'Access millions of teacher-created resources' could describe any EdTech platform. One concrete feature callout (e.g., 'Wayground's AI activity generator lets you tier a task across Access / On-level / Extension in under 5 minutes') would fix this entirely.

C2 (Opening Conciseness) scores a 3: The article opens with a rhetorical reframe — 'Mixed-ability classes aren't your teaching challenge; they're exactly why differentiated instruction works so well' — which is mildly catchy but still throat-clearing. A teacher scanning at 6am wants the payoff immediately. Starting instead with 'Here is a six-step workflow for differentiating in mixed-ability classrooms without doubling your prep time' would earn a 5.

C9 (Standards Alignment) scores a 3: For a lesson-design article of this depth — covering tiered tasks, formative assessment, UDL-adjacent scaffolding — there is no named framework anywhere. A single line connecting the three-lever model (content/process/product) to UDL Principle 1, or noting that ISTE Standard 5 (Designer) maps to tiered task design, would lift this to a 4–5 and add credibility for teachers who need to justify their methods to administrators.
positive_signals: The tiered task table (Access / On-level / Extension with Teacher Move column) is genuinely photocopiable — this is exactly the kind of artifact a department head would laminate and hand to a new teacher. It earns its 5 on visual structure.

Grade-band specificity is unusually strong: the 6th-grade math ratio example, the 7th-grade Title I science teacher with IEP students, and the middle-school framing throughout give the article real classroom texture that most differentiation content lacks.
narrative: This article is one of the stronger differentiation pieces a K-12 teacher is likely to find online — the six-step workflow is genuinely usable on Monday, the tiered task table is reference-quality, and the grade-specific vignettes give it real classroom credibility. The single issue that triggers a REVISE is the Wayground CTA section, which names no specific product features and reads identically to how any EdTech company would describe itself ("built-in accommodations," "millions of resources") — fix it by naming one actual Wayground feature and its time-saving mechanism, e.g., "Wayground's AI activity generator lets you produce tiered Access/On-level/Extension versions of any task in under five minutes." While you're in revision, swap the opener's rhetorical reframe for a direct one-sentence payoff, and add a single named standards framework (UDL, ISTE, or Common Core anchor standards) to support teachers who need to justify their practice to administrators. Those three targeted edits would push this to a clean PASS.
